calendar.matters:
CASE MANAGEMENT CONFERENCE
Off Calendar Mar-16-2026 Continued To Jul-01-26 At 10:30 A.M. In Dept 610

DEMURRER to Amended COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Mar-11-2026 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: DEFENDANT HYUNDAI MOTOR AMERICA'S DEMURRER TO PLAINTIFFS' FIRST AMENDED COMPLAINT

Appearances:
Rohit Sengupta of the Theta Law Firm (424.297.3103, eservice@thetafirm.com) for defendant and moving party;
Angel Baker of Strategic Legal Practice (310.929.4900, abaker@slpattorney.com) for plaintiff and responding party

Argued and the court adopts its tentative ruling as follows: Defendant Hyundai Motor America's demurrer to plaintiffs Arnold Ramos et al.'s cause of action for fraudulent concealment is overruled.

"A plaintiff may assert a tort claim for fraudulent concealment based on conduct occurring in the course of a contractual relationship, if the elements of the cause of action can be established independently of the parties' contractual rights and obligations and the tortious conduct exposes the plaintiff to a risk of harm beyond the reasonable contemplation of the parties when they entered into the agreement." (Rattagan v. Uber Technologies, Inc. (2024) 17 Cal.5th 1, 38.) At the pleadings stage, plaintiffs have pled facts that satisfy these elements: they plead that Hyundai was aware of and concealed its knowledge of a dangerous battery in the car Ramos purchased (FAC, paras. 52-54), and they plead that the battery exposed them to risks that ordinary consumers would not expect in operating their car, including the risk of overheating and spontaneous combustion (id., para. 50).

Hyundai also demurs on the basis that the fraudulent concealment cause of action is pled with insufficient particularity. The elements of this claim are "(1) misrepresentation (false representation, concealment, or nondisclosure); (2) knowledge of falsity (scienter); (3) intent to defraud (i.e., to induce reliance); (4) justifiable reliance; and (5) resulting damage." (Dhital v. Nissan North America, Inc. (2022) 84 Cal.App.5th 828, 845 (Dhital). Hyundai correctly argues that fraud must be pleaded with particularity. (See Stansfield v. Starkey (1990) 220 Cal.App.3d 59, 74.)

The court concludes that the pleading is adequate here in light of Dhital, a case considering the adequacy of similar pleadings. In Dhital, plaintiffs brought a Song-Beverly Act claim against a car manufacturer. Along with Song-Beverly claims, they alleged "that Nissan, by intentionally concealing facts about the defective transmission, fraudulently induced them to purchase a car." (Dhital, 84 Cal.App.5th at p. 838.) The court of appeal held that plaintiffs adequately pleaded their fraudulent inducement claim by pleading that "the CVT transmissions installed in numerous Nissan vehicles (including the one plaintiffs purchased) were defective; Nissan knew of the defects and the hazards they posed; Nissan had exclusive knowledge of the defects but intentionally concealed and failed to disclose that information; Nissan intended to deceive plaintiffs by concealing known transmission problems; plaintiffs would not have purchased the car if they had known of the defects; and plaintiffs suffered damages in the form of money paid to purchase the car." (Id. at p. 844.) Plaintiff's fraudulent inducement claims contain analogous allegations at similar levels of detail. (FAC, paras 50-59.)

Prevailing party to submit a proposed form of order. Judge: Christine Van Aken, Clerk: E. Larnauti, Not Reported (301/CVA)

MOTION TO STRIKE Amended COMPLAINT

MINI MINUTES: DEFENDANT'S MOTION TO STRIKE PORTIONS OF PLAINTIFFS' FIRST AMENDED COMPLAINT

MOTION TO STRIKE Amended COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Mar-11-2026 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: DEFENDANT HYUNDAI MOTOR AMERICA'S MOTION TO STRIKE PORTIONS OF PLAINTIFFS' FIRST AMENDED COMPLAINT

Appearances:
Rohit Sengupta of the Theta Law Firm (424.297.3103, eservice@thetafirm.com) for defendant and moving party;
Angel Baker of Strategic Legal Practice (310.929.4900, abaker@slpattorney.com) for plaintiff and responding party

Argued and the court adopts its tentative ruling as follows: Defendant Hyundai Motor America's motion to strike the First Amended Complaint's punitive damages prayer is denied. By companion order today, the court has overruled the demurrer to plaintiffs Arnold Ramos et al.'s fraudulent concealment cause of action. A fraudulent concealment cause of action may give rise to punitive damages. Here, Ramos alleges that the fraudulent concealment exposed him to serious safety hazards, including the including the risk of overheating and spontaneous combustion in operating the car. (FAC, para. 50.) The court concludes that plaintiffs have adequately alleged facts giving rise to a claim for punitive damages.

Prevailing party to submit a proposed form of order. Judge: Christine Van Aken, Clerk: E. Larnauti, Not Reported (301/CVA)
